description: Disturb Opponents' No Trump with 2-level overcalls showing specific suit patterns. VARIANT: this fixture encodes the modern DONT variant where 2S is a natural single-suit spade overcall as a direct overcall, and module.dont.singleSuited explicitly excludes spades. The original Bergen version treats spades as a single-suit type revealed via takeout double (a reveal-spades-2S surface under wait-reveal). Verify runs that anchor on the original Bergen authority will flag a missing reveal-spades-2S surface — this is intentional. See fixtures/modules/dont.variant-notes.md for details.
purpose: Compete against an opponent's 1NT opening by describing your distribution cheaply, letting partner judge fit and level

authority.label: Bridge Guys — comprehensive DONT coverage including Marty Bergen's original and common variants

variantNotes.label: Modern vs. original Bergen DONT variant selection — documents why this fixture has no reveal-spades-2S surface

teaching.tradeoff: DONT overcalls commit to the 2-level immediately — if partner has a misfit, you may go down.
teaching.principle: Against a strong 1NT, disrupting their communication is more valuable than finding your own perfect contract.

commonMistakes.text: Do not use DONT with only four-card suits at unfavorable vulnerability.
commonMistakes.reason: The method works best with shapely playing strength; flimsy four-carders do not survive the relay-and-correct auction well.

commonMistakes.text: Do not compete with DONT on a flat 8-count and no real long suit.
commonMistakes.reason: If you are not showing a genuine one-suiter or two-suiter, you help declarer more than partner.

commonMistakes.text: Do not treat this fixture's 2♠ call as Bergen's spades-plus-a-minor agreement.
commonMistakes.reason: This module uses a natural 6+ spade overcall, so partnerships expecting the two-suited 2♠ treatment will be on different continuations immediately.

explanationEntries.displayText: Double: One long suit (6+), relay for identity
Pass: No DONT action (hand does not qualify)
2S: Prefer spades (3+ spades, fewer than 3 hearts)
3C: Escape to long clubs (6+), no major fit
3D: Escape to long diamonds (6+), no major fit
Pass: Accept diamonds as trump (3+ diamonds)
2H: Relay asking which major partner holds
Pass: Accept clubs as trump (3+ clubs)
2D: Relay asking which higher suit partner holds
Pass: Accept spades as trump (3+ spades)
Pass: Accept spades by default (no better option)
Pass: Higher suit is diamonds (already bid via relay)
Pass: The major is hearts (already bid via relay)

summaryCard.trigger: Opponent opens one notrump and you interfere
summaryCard.partnership: This fixture treats DONT as a defense to a strong one notrump and uses the natural two-spades treatment, where two spades shows a six-card or longer spade suit. Some partnerships prefer Marty Bergen's original two-suited style where two spades shows spades plus an unspecified minor, so confirm that agreement before playing.

whenToUse.gloss: Use the double with a single long suit when the cheapest relay will let partner uncover which suit you own.
Use the artificial two-level overcalls to show clubs-plus-higher or diamonds-plus-a-major when describing two suits cheaply matters more than bidding one naturally.
Use two hearts for both majors, or bid two spades naturally with a real spade suit, when the majors are where you expect to compete.

workedAuctions.label: Double then relay reveals the long suit

calls.rationale: DONT double shows a single long suit rather than penalties.
The opening side does not compete immediately.
Advancer makes the forced relay so overcaller can identify the long suit.
Overcaller corrects to hearts, completing the DONT story.

workedAuctions.label: Counter-example: do not treat the double as penalty

calls.rationale: In DONT, this announces a single-suited hand.
Passing here would be the wrong partnership expectation, because advancer is supposed to relay unless holding a special agreement.

items.opponentAction: The opener's side doubles an artificial two clubs, two diamonds, or two hearts call
items.ourAction: Pass with tolerance, redouble to ask for the other suit, or compete naturally with a long suit of your own.
items.note: The fixture includes dedicated competitive continuations after doubles of the artificial calls.

items.opponentAction: The opener's side bids a new suit over the artificial call
items.ourAction: Double to ask partner to act with the right holding, or bid your own long suit naturally if that tells the story better.
items.note: Once the opponents consume space, the cheapest natural escape often matters more than repeating the full DONT structure.

items.recommendation: With a weak hand, pass or correct into the known fit, and use the cheapest relay only when partner still owes shape.
items.note: This strength ladder replaces the old call-by-call chart because the hidden two-suited shape partition is still deferred.

items.recommendation: With invitational values, keep competing naturally unless partner's artificial call still needs a relay to uncover the second suit.
items.note: The shared bucket loses the exact mapping from each artificial overcall to its promised shape.

items.recommendation: With game values, use the forcing inquiry or jump once you know enough about the fit.
items.note: The game-forcing branch stays visible, but the old per-call rows are gone until overcall.twoSuitedShape exists.

relatedLinks.discriminator: Both conventions trade natural calls for fast two-suit disclosure, but DONT applies over one notrump while Michaels/Unusual applies over suit openings.

definitions.description: H5+S4+ or S5+H4+ — both majors for 2H bid
D5+ and (H4+ or S4+) — diamonds + a major for 2D bid
C5+ and (D4+ or H4+ or S4+) — clubs + higher suit for 2C bid
S6+ — natural spades for 2S bid
One suit 6+, no other suit 4+, longest suit is not spades — for double
D6+ — long diamonds for bypass/escape
H6+ — long hearts for bypass/escape
S6+ — long spades for bypass/escape
C6+ — long clubs for escape
8-10 HCP — minimum DONT range for forcing-inquiry replies
11-15 HCP — maximum DONT range for forcing-inquiry replies
With diamonds and a major, the major is longer than diamonds
With both majors, spades are longer than hearts
The 6+ single suit is clubs
The 6+ single suit is diamonds
The 6+ single suit is hearts
With clubs as anchor, higher suit is diamonds
With clubs as anchor, higher suit is hearts
With clubs as anchor, higher suit is spades
With diamonds as anchor, the major is hearts
With diamonds as anchor, the major is spades
3+ hearts, or equal length in both majors (2-2)
3+ spades
3+ diamonds
3+ clubs
6+ in clubs or diamonds (for minor escape)
Longer minor is clubs (for 3C escape)
Longer minor is diamonds (for 3D escape)
